perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of the State of California without regard to its conflict-of-laws principles. The federal and state courts within the State of California will have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement. Each party irrevocably consents to the personal jurisdiction of such courts and expressly waives any objection to such jurisdiction based on inconvenient forum or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of a jurisdiction chosen by the Company. The courts within that jurisdiction will have jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement. Each party consents to the jurisdiction of such courts.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specification of California law and exclusive jurisdiction, along with the waiver of objection to jurisdiction, weakens consumer protections available under California law and allows the company to choose a jurisdiction potentially less favorable to the consumer.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CCRA provides specific protections to California consumers, including rights related to deceptive practices. By removing the governing law as California and specifying the company can choose any jurisdiction, consumers are potentially stripped of these protections, thus contradicting the intent and purpose of the CCRA.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies California law as the governing law and exclusive jurisdiction for disputes. The changed text removes this specification, allowing the company to choose the governing law and jurisdiction. According to the Law Explanation, this could strip consumers of protections under the CCRA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the Consumers Legal Remedies Act (CCRA), indicating that California law provides specific consumer protections. By omitting the specification of California law, the changed text potentially contradicts the protections afforded to consumers under California law, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each disclosing party acknowledges that it has attempted in good faith to provide accurate information to the receiving party. Notwithstanding that attempt, the disclosing party makes no representations or warranties, express or implied, to the receiving party regarding, and the receiving party may not rely on, the completeness or accuracy of any information provided to the receiving party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each disclosing party makes no representations or warranties, express or implied, to the receiving party regarding, and the receiving party may not rely on, the completeness or accuracy of any information provided to the receiving party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the initial statement about attempting in good faith to provide accurate information, the modified clause creates a loophole that could shield the disclosing party from liability even if they knowingly provide inaccurate information, conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing inherent in contracts under California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code - Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 1201(b)(20) defines good faith as 'honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing.' Omitting the acknowledgment of an attempt to provide accurate information undermines this duty, as it suggests a willingness to provide information without regard for its accuracy, potentially violating the requirement of honesty in fact and fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 1201 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 1. GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person
perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'good faith' attempt acknowledgement creates a contradiction. The Law Explanation points to California Commercial Code § 1201(b)(20), defining good faith as 'honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing.' By removing the original clause, the disclosing party is no longer held to the standard of attempting to provide accurate information, which can be interpreted as a departure from 'honesty in fact', thus creating a potential legal loophole where inaccurate information could be provided without accountability. Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides the broader context of COM 1201 within the California Commercial Code, reinforcing its relevance. While the snippet itself does not directly state the 'good faith' definition, it establishes the section under which such definitions are provided, supporting the importance of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: Company is aware, and will advise its Representatives who are provided any Confidential Information of ZipRealty, of the restrictions imposed by federal securities laws on the purchase or sale of ZipRealty’s securities by any person who has received material non-public information from ZipRealty or its Representatives and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person may purchase or sell ZipRealty’s securities while in possession of such information. Company agrees to require its Representatives to comply with these restrictions.
perturbation.changed_text: Company is aware of the restrictions imposed by federal securities laws on the purchase or sale of ZipRealty’s securities by any person who has received material non-public information from ZipRealty or its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit obligation for the Company to advise its Representatives about insider trading restrictions and to ensure their compliance weakens the protection against insider trading. This creates legal risk by failing to actively prevent violations of securities laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Insider Trading
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices, including insider trading.  While the company is aware of the law, removing the duty to advise its representatives and ensure compliance reduces the preventative measures to ensure compliance with these laws, which introduces legal risk.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states the Company's obligation to advise its Representatives about insider trading restrictions and to ensure their compliance. The changed text removes this explicit obligation, only stating the Company is aware of the restrictions. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this weakens the protection against insider trading. The Law Explanation states that removing the duty to advise representatives reduces preventative measures to ensure compliance with securities laws. Snippet 1, 15 USC 78j, prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices, including insider trading. By removing the obligation to actively inform and ensure compliance, the modified contract introduces legal risk, making it more likely that insider trading violations could occur. This constitutes a contradiction because it weakens preventative measures designed to comply with the law against manipulative devices in securities trading.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that its obligations hereunder are necessary and reasonable in order to protect the other party and the other party’s business and expressly agrees that monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate the other party for any breach by either party of any covenants and agreements set forth herein. Accordingly, each party agrees and acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation may cause irreparable injury to the other party and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in law, in equity or otherwise, the other party will be entitled to seek injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the necessity of proving actual damages.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that monetary damages will be an adequate remedy for any breach of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the standard legal principle that injunctive relief is often available in cases where monetary damages are inadequate, particularly in cases involving confidential information. By stating monetary damages are always adequate, it limits the remedies available and potentially harms the non-breaching party, particularly ZipRealty.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3422 allows for injunctive relief when monetary damages would not afford adequate relief.  By stating that monetary damages are always an adequate remedy, the modified text attempts to waive the right to seek injunctive relief, which can be a crucial remedy for protecting confidential information. This creates a legal contradiction with established legal principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3422. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Preventive Relief [3420 - 3424] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 3422. Except where otherwise provided by this Title, a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; 2. Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief; 3. Where the restraint is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings; or, 4. Where the obligation arises from a trust. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text states that monetary damages will always be an adequate remedy, directly contradicting California Civil Code § 3422. The scraped law snippet states that injunctive relief may be granted where pecuniary compensation (monetary damages) would not afford adequate relief. By stating monetary damages are *always* adequate, the modified contract eliminates the possibility of seeking injunctive relief, a legally recognized remedy under specific circumstances.
